flashcards.definition: A location in space with no size or dimension; represented by a dot.
An infinite set of points extending in two directions; no width or thickness.
A flat surface extending infinitely in all directions; defined by three non-collinear points.
Points that lie on the same line.
Points or lines that lie in the same plane.
A part of a line with two endpoints and a definite length.
A part of a line with one endpoint extending infinitely in one direction.
The degree of exactness of a measurement; depends on the measuring tool.
Point B is between A and C if A, B, C are collinear and AB + BC = AC.
Segments with equal lengths; denoted by ≅.
d = √[(x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)²]; finds the distance between two points on a coordinate plane.
M = ((x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2); the point exactly halfway between two endpoints.
A point, line, or ray that divides a segment into two congruent parts.
Used in the ruler postulate: AB = |a − b| where a and b are coordinates on a number line.
Distance = |coordinate₁ − coordinate₂|.
A figure formed by two rays sharing a common endpoint (vertex).
The common endpoint of two rays forming an angle.
An angle measuring between 0° and 90°.
An angle measuring exactly 90°.
An angle measuring between 90° and 180°.
Two angles whose measures sum to 90°.
Two angles whose measures sum to 180°.
Opposite angles formed by two intersecting lines; always congruent.
Two adjacent angles that form a straight line; supplementary (sum = 180°).
Angles that share a common vertex and side but don't overlap.
A closed plane figure formed by three or more line segments (sides).
A polygon with all sides and angles congruent.
All interior angles are less than 180°; no sides 'cave in.'
At least one interior angle is greater than 180°.
The total distance around a polygon; sum of all side lengths.
A three-dimensional figure with flat polygonal faces.
A polyhedron with two parallel, congruent bases connected by rectangles.
A polyhedron with one polygonal base and triangular lateral faces meeting at an apex.
A 3D figure with two parallel circular bases connected by a curved surface.
The set of all points equidistant from a center point in 3D space.
The set of all points equidistant from a center point; distance = radius.
A segment from the center to any point on the circle.
A chord passing through the center; d = 2r.
C = 2πr = πd; the distance around a circle.
A segment with both endpoints on the circle.
An angle with its vertex at the center of the circle.
A portion of the circle between two points.
An arc less than 180°; named by two letters.
An arc greater than 180°; named by three letters.
The measure of a minor arc equals the measure of its central angle.
The distance along an arc; s = (θ/360°) × 2πr.
Arcs with equal measures in the same or congruent circles.
Congruent chords have congruent arcs, and vice versa.
Chords equidistant from the center are congruent.
Bisects the chord and its arc.
An angle with its vertex on the circle; formed by two chords.
An inscribed angle is half the measure of its intercepted arc.
The arc that lies in the interior of an inscribed angle.
An angle inscribed in a semicircle is 90°.
Opposite angles of a quadrilateral inscribed in a circle are supplementary.
A line that touches the circle at exactly one point.
The point where a tangent meets the circle.
A tangent is perpendicular to the radius at the point of tangency.
Two tangent segments from the same external point are congruent.
A line tangent to two circles (external or internal).

u10_l10.6.title: Secants, Tangents, and Angle Measures

flashcards.definition: A line that intersects a circle at two points.
Angle = ½|arc₁ − arc₂| when vertex is outside the circle.
Angle = ½|arc₁ − arc₂| with vertex outside.
Angle = ½|major arc − minor arc|.
Angle = ½(arc₁ + arc₂) when two chords intersect inside the circle.

u10_l10.7.title: Special Segments in a Circle

flashcards.definition: If two chords intersect inside: (seg₁)(seg₂) = (seg₃)(seg₄).
From outside: (whole₁)(external₁) = (whole₂)(external₂).
(whole secant)(external) = (tangent)².
The common value of these products for a given point.
Finding unknown segment lengths using these relationships.
(x − h)² + (y − k)² = r²; center (h, k), radius r.
x² + y² + Dx + Ey + F = 0; convert by completing the square.
Technique to rewrite general form into standard form.
Read directly from standard form: center = (h, k), radius = √(r²).
Plot center, mark radius in all directions, draw smooth curve.
Curves formed by a plane intersecting a cone: circle, ellipse, parabola, hyperbola.
An oval shape defined by two foci; sum of distances from any point to foci is constant.
The set of points equidistant from a focus and a directrix.
Two open curves; difference of distances from any point to two foci is constant.
Point, line, or intersecting lines formed when the plane passes through the apex.
A = bh; base × height.
A = ½bh; half base × height.
Height is always perpendicular to the base.
The perpendicular distance from the base to the opposite vertex (= height).
Area is measured in square units (cm², m², in², ft²).

u11_l11.2.title: Areas of Trapezoids, Rhombi, and Kites

flashcards.definition: A = ½(b₁ + b₂)h; average of bases × height.
A = ½d₁d₂; half the product of the diagonals.
A = ½d₁d₂; same formula as rhombus.
The perpendicular distance between the two parallel bases.
The two parallel sides.
A = πr².
A 'pie slice' of a circle bounded by two radii and an arc.
Region between a chord and its arc; area = sector area − triangle area.
The region between two concentric circles; area = π(R² − r²).

u11_l11.4.title: Areas of Regular Polygons and Composite Figures

flashcards.definition: A = ½ap; apothem × perimeter / 2.
The perpendicular distance from the center to the midpoint of a side.
A figure made up of two or more basic shapes.
Break into basic shapes, find each area, then add (or subtract).
p = n × s; number of sides × side length.
If scale factor is k, area ratio is k².
If scale factor is k, perimeter ratio is k.
If two figures are similar with known scale factor, A₂ = k² × A₁.
Same shape, proportional sides — areas scale by the square of the side ratio.
Scaling models: double the dimensions → quadruple the area.
Area under a curve found by summing infinitely thin rectangles.
Estimate curved areas using inscribed or circumscribed rectangles.
Approximation: Σ f(xᵢ)Δx.
Increasing the number of rectangles improves the approximation.
The definite integral gives the exact area under a curve.
A 2D pattern that folds into a 3D figure.
Views of a 3D object from the front, top, and side.
A 3D representation on a flat surface showing three faces.
The shape formed when a plane intersects a solid.
V − E + F = 2; relates vertices, edges, and faces of polyhedra.

u12_l12.2.title: Surface Areas of Prisms and Cylinders

flashcards.definition: SA = 2B + Ph; two bases + lateral area.
LA = Ph; perimeter of base × height.
SA = 2πr² + 2πrh.
LA = 2πrh; the curved surface 'unrolled' is a rectangle.
In a right prism/cylinder, the lateral faces are perpendicular to the bases.

u12_l12.3.title: Surface Areas of Pyramids and Cones

flashcards.definition: SA = B + ½Pl; base area + half perimeter × slant height.
The distance from the apex to the midpoint of a base edge (along a face).
SA = πr² + πrl; base + lateral.
LA = πrl; where l is the slant height.
l = √(r² + h²); related by the Pythagorean theorem.
V = Bh; base area × height.
V = πr²h.
The area of the base shape (rectangle, triangle, circle, etc.).
The perpendicular distance between the two bases.
Measured in cubic units (cm³, m³, in³, ft³).
V = ⅓Bh; one-third base area × height.
V = ⅓πr²h.
A pyramid/cone has ⅓ the volume of a prism/cylinder with the same base and height.
Same volume formula applies — Cavalieri's Principle.
Calculating volumes of roofs, ice cream cones, pyramids.

u12_l12.6.title: Surface Areas and Volumes of Spheres

flashcards.definition: SA = 4πr².
V = ⁴⁄₃πr³.
The cross section with the largest area; passes through the center. A = πr².
Half a sphere; V = ⅔πr³, SA = 3πr².
Calculating volumes of balls, globes, tanks.
Geometry on the surface of a sphere; 'lines' are great circles.
The shortest path between two points on a sphere (arc of a great circle).
A triangle on a sphere bounded by arcs of great circles; angle sum > 180°.
Spherical geometry violates Euclid's parallel postulate (no parallel lines exist).
Navigation, air travel routes, GPS systems.
Solids with the same shape and size — all corresponding dimensions are equal.
Solids with the same shape but possibly different size; corresponding ratios are equal.
If scale factor is k, SA ratio is k².
If scale factor is k, volume ratio is k³.
Doubling all dimensions: SA × 4, Volume × 8.
If two solids have equal cross-sectional areas at every height, they have equal volumes.
The area of the shape formed when a plane cuts through a solid.
Same volume as right prism/cylinder with same base and height (Cavalieri).
Justifies volume formulas for oblique figures.
Compare cross sections of two solids at each height to prove equal volumes.
The set of all possible outcomes of an experiment.
A subset of the sample space — a specific outcome or set of outcomes.
A single possible result of an experiment.
A visual showing all possible outcomes using branching paths.
If event A has m outcomes and event B has n, there are m × n total outcomes.
An arrangement where order matters: P(n, r) = n!/(n−r)!
A selection where order does NOT matter: C(n, r) = n!/[r!(n−r)!].
When the ORDER of selection matters (rankings, passwords).
When only the GROUP matters, not the order (committees, lottery).
Probability based on areas, lengths, or volumes rather than counting outcomes.
P = favorable length / total length.
P = favorable area / total area.
A point chosen randomly, with equal likelihood anywhere in the region.
Dart boards, landing zones, spinner problems.
A model of a real-world situation used to estimate probabilities.
A tool that produces unpredictable numbers for simulations.
One complete run of a simulation.
P = (# times event occurs) / (total # of trials).
Theoretical is calculated; experimental is observed from data.

u13_l13.5.title: Probabilities of Independent and Dependent Events

flashcards.definition: The outcome of one event does NOT affect the other; P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B).
The outcome of one affects the other; P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B|A).
P(B|A): the probability of B given that A has occurred.
After drawing, the item is returned — events are independent.
After drawing, the item is NOT returned — events are dependent.
Events that cannot happen at the same time; P(A and B) = 0.
P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B).
P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A and B).
Events that CAN occur simultaneously; must subtract the overlap.
P(A') = 1 − P(A); the probability of 'not A.'
Using random sampling to estimate mathematical results.
Inscribe circle in square; ratio of hits inside circle ≈ π/4.
Randomly scatter points; ratio of favorable points ≈ ratio of areas.
More trials give estimates closer to the true value (Law of Large Numbers).
Used in physics, finance, and computer science for complex probability problems.
Drawing general conclusions from specific observations or patterns.
An unproven statement based on observations.
A single example that disproves a conjecture.
Identifying a consistent rule from repeated observations.
An ordered list of numbers following a pattern.
A sentence that is either true or false, but not both.
The opposite of a statement; denoted ¬p or ~p.
A compound statement using 'and' (p ∧ q); true only if both parts are true.
A compound statement using 'or' (p ∨ q); true if at least one part is true.
A table showing all possible truth values for a logical expression.
An 'if-then' statement: p → q (hypothesis → conclusion).
Formed by switching the hypothesis and conclusion: q → p.
Negating both parts: ¬p → ¬q.
Negating and switching: ¬q → ¬p. Always has the same truth value as the original.
'If and only if' (p ↔ q); true when both parts have the same truth value.
Using logic and established facts to reach a specific conclusion.
If p → q is true and p is true, then q is true.
If p → q and q → r, then p → r.
A logical argument where the conclusion follows necessarily from the premises.
A statement that has been proven true using deductive reasoning.
A statement accepted as true without proof (axiom).
A proof written as a logical paragraph, citing postulates, definitions, and theorems.
The information provided at the start of a proof — the known facts.
The statement you must demonstrate to be true at the end of the proof.
Definitions can always be written as 'if and only if' statements.
A proof using algebraic properties (addition, subtraction, substitution, etc.).
a = a; any quantity is equal to itself.
If a = b, then b = a.
If a = b and b = c, then a = c.
If a = b, then a can replace b in any expression.
If B is between A and C, then AB + BC = AC.
If M is the midpoint of AB, then AM = MB.
A proof with numbered statements in one column and reasons in another.
Segments are congruent if and only if they have equal measures.
Points on a line can be paired with real numbers, and distance = |a − b|.
If D is in the interior of ∠ABC, then m∠ABD + m∠DBC = m∠ABC.
If two angles are supplementary to the same angle, they're congruent.
If two angles are complementary to the same angle, they're congruent.
Vertical angles are congruent.
All right angles are congruent.
A proof using the coordinate plane, placing figures on axes and using formulas.
Strategically place shapes at the origin or along axes to simplify proofs.
Used to show segments are congruent (equal length).
Used to show lines are parallel (equal slopes) or perpendicular (negative reciprocal slopes).
Used to show a point bisects a segment.
A line that intersects two or more lines at different points.
Angles in the same position at each intersection; congruent if lines are parallel.
Angles between the lines on opposite sides of the transversal; congruent if parallel.
Angles outside the lines on opposite sides of the transversal; congruent if parallel.
Angles between the lines on the same side; supplementary if parallel.
Coplanar lines that never intersect; have equal slopes.
If a transversal cuts parallel lines, corresponding angles are congruent.
If a transversal cuts parallel lines, alternate interior angles are congruent.
If a transversal cuts parallel lines, same-side interior angles are supplementary.
If a transversal is perpendicular to one parallel line, it's perpendicular to the other.
Rise over run: m = (y₂ − y₁)/(x₂ − x₁).
Parallel lines have identical slopes (m₁ = m₂).
Perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals (m₁ · m₂ = −1).
A horizontal line has slope 0.
A vertical line has undefined slope.
y = mx + b; m = slope, b = y-intercept.
y − y₁ = m(x − x₁); used when slope and one point are known.
Ax + By = C; where A, B, C are integers and A > 0.
The point where a line crosses the y-axis (0, b).
The point where a line crosses the x-axis (x, 0).
If corresponding angles are congruent, then the lines are parallel.
If alternate interior angles are congruent, the lines are parallel.
If co-interior angles are supplementary, the lines are parallel.
Show that angle pairs formed by a transversal satisfy parallel line theorems.
A proof that uses arrows to show the logical flow from given to conclusion.
Lines that intersect at 90° angles.
The length of the perpendicular segment from the point to the line.
A line perpendicular to a segment at its midpoint.
Measured along a perpendicular to both lines; constant everywhere.
Using compass and straightedge to draw a perpendicular from a point to a line.
Using coordinates and algebra to prove geometric properties.
Show two lines have equal slopes to prove they're parallel.
Show slopes are negative reciprocals to prove perpendicularity.
Verify properties of geometric figures placed on the coordinate plane.
Find the midpoint and negative reciprocal slope, then write the equation.
No sides congruent.
At least two sides congruent.
All three sides congruent (also equiangular: all angles 60°).
All angles are less than 90°.
One angle is greater than 90°.
The sum of interior angles of any triangle is 180°.
An exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent interior angles.
An angle formed by one side and the extension of an adjacent side.
The two interior angles not adjacent to a given exterior angle.
The acute angles of a right triangle are complementary.
Triangles with all corresponding sides and angles congruent.
Corresponding Parts of Congruent Triangles are Congruent.
Sides and angles in the same relative position in congruent figures.
△ABC ≅ △DEF means A↔D, B↔E, C↔F for both angles and sides.
If two angles of one triangle are congruent to two of another, the third angles are congruent.
If three sides of one triangle are congruent to three sides of another, the triangles are congruent.
If two sides and the included angle are congruent, the triangles are congruent.
The angle formed between two given sides of a triangle.
A transformation (translation, rotation, reflection) that preserves size and shape.
A triangle is the only polygon determined by its side lengths.
If two angles and the included side are congruent, the triangles are congruent.
If two angles and a non-included side are congruent, the triangles are congruent.
The side between two given angles of a triangle.
For right triangles: if hypotenuse and one leg are congruent, the triangles are congruent.
Two sides and a non-included angle do NOT guarantee congruence (ambiguous case).
If two sides of a triangle are congruent, the base angles are congruent.
If two angles are congruent, the sides opposite them are congruent.
A triangle is equilateral if and only if it is equiangular (each 60°).
The two angles adjacent to the base of an isosceles triangle.
The angle opposite the base in an isosceles triangle.
A rigid motion that maps one figure onto a congruent figure.
Slides a figure without rotating or reflecting it.
Turns a figure around a fixed point (center of rotation).
Flips a figure over a line (line of reflection).
Performing two or more transformations in sequence.
Placing a triangle on the coordinate plane and using formulas to prove properties.
Set one vertex at the origin and one side along an axis to simplify calculations.
Use variables (a, b) for coordinates to prove general statements.
Show a segment connects midpoints or bisects another.
Show sides are congruent by computing distances.
A segment perpendicular to a side at its midpoint.
The point where the three perpendicular bisectors meet; equidistant from all three vertices.
A ray that divides an angle into two congruent angles.
The point where the three angle bisectors meet; equidistant from all three sides.
A circle passing through all three vertices of a triangle; center = circumcenter.
A segment from a vertex to the midpoint of the opposite side.
The point where the three medians meet; divides each median in a 2:1 ratio.
A perpendicular segment from a vertex to the line containing the opposite side.
The point where the three altitudes meet.
A line through the circumcenter, centroid, and orthocenter of a triangle.
In a triangle, the longer side is opposite the larger angle.
In a triangle, the larger angle is opposite the longer side.
An exterior angle is greater than either non-adjacent interior angle.
Compare angle measures to rank sides, or compare sides to rank angles.
Opposite the largest angle.
Proof by contradiction: assume the negation, derive a contradiction, conclude the original is true.
Another name for indirect proof.
The opposite of the statement to be proven.
A logical impossibility that arises from the false assumption.
When a direct proof is hard — especially for 'not' or inequality statements.
The sum of any two sides must be greater than the third side.
Check all three pairs: a + b > c, a + c > b, b + c > a.
A straight line — the triangle inequality in action.
If a + b = c exactly, the 'triangle' is a straight line (not a valid triangle).
Determine whether three given lengths can form a triangle.
If two sides of one △ are ≅ to two sides of another, the larger included angle → longer third side.
If two sides of one △ are ≅ to two sides of another and the third sides differ, the larger third side → larger included angle.
Two triangles with two pairs of congruent sides; compare third side or included angle.
Determine which triangle has the longer side or larger angle.
Often uses indirect proof or the properties of isosceles triangles.
(n − 2) × 180°; the sum of interior angles of an n-sided polygon.
Always 360° for any convex polygon.
Each angle = (n − 2) × 180° / n.
A segment connecting two non-adjacent vertices.
n(n − 3)/2 diagonals in an n-sided polygon.
A quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides parallel.
Opposite sides of a parallelogram are congruent.
Opposite angles of a parallelogram are congruent.
Consecutive angles of a parallelogram are supplementary.
The diagonals of a parallelogram bisect each other.
If both pairs of opposite sides are congruent, the quad is a parallelogram.
If the diagonals bisect each other, the quad is a parallelogram.
If one pair of sides is both parallel and congruent, it's a parallelogram.
If both pairs of opposite angles are congruent, it's a parallelogram.
Show both pairs of opposite sides have equal slopes and equal lengths.
A parallelogram with four right angles.
Diagonals of a rectangle are congruent (and bisect each other).
A parallelogram with one right angle is a rectangle.
A parallelogram with congruent diagonals is a rectangle.
Opposite sides ≅, opposite angles ≅, diagonals ≅ and bisect each other.
A parallelogram with all four sides congruent.
Diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular and bisect each other.
A rectangle AND a rhombus — all sides ≅, all angles 90°, diagonals ≅ and ⊥.
Each diagonal of a rhombus bisects a pair of opposite angles.
A parallelogram with one pair of consecutive sides congruent is a rhombus.
A quadrilateral with exactly one pair of parallel sides (bases).
A trapezoid with congruent legs; base angles are congruent, diagonals are congruent.
Connects the midpoints of the legs; parallel to bases and length = (b₁ + b₂)/2.
A quadrilateral with two pairs of consecutive congruent sides (no pair of opposite sides ≅).
Perpendicular; one diagonal bisects the other.
All sides and all angles are congruent; both equilateral and equiangular.
A regular n-gon has n lines of symmetry.
A regular n-gon has rotational symmetry of 360°/n.
The perpendicular distance from the center to the midpoint of a side.
Equidistant from all vertices and all sides.
A comparison of two quantities: a/b or a:b.
An equation stating two ratios are equal: a/b = c/d.
In a proportion a/b = c/d, ad = bc.
In a:b = c:d, b and c are means; a and d are extremes.
If a/b = c/d, then ad = bc, a/c = b/d, and (a+b)/b = (c+d)/d.
Polygons with the same shape: corresponding angles ≅ and corresponding sides proportional.
The ratio of corresponding side lengths of similar polygons.
△ABC ~ △DEF; order indicates corresponding vertices.
The ratio of perimeters equals the scale factor.
Similar = same shape, possibly different size. Congruent = same shape AND size.
If two angles of one triangle are congruent to two of another, the triangles are similar.
If all three pairs of corresponding sides are proportional, the triangles are similar.
If two pairs of sides are proportional and the included angles are congruent, similar.
Using similar triangles to calculate distances that can't be measured directly.
Using similar triangles formed by shadows to find heights.
If a line parallel to one side of a triangle intersects the other two sides, it divides them proportionally.
If a line divides two sides proportionally, it's parallel to the third side.
A midsegment is parallel to the third side and half its length.
If three parallels cut two transversals, they divide them proportionally.
Parallel lines create proportional segments on transversals.
An angle bisector divides the opposite side proportionally to the adjacent sides.
In a right triangle, the altitude to the hypotenuse creates two similar triangles.
In a right triangle with altitude h to hypotenuse c: h² = (segment₁)(segment₂).
Corresponding altitudes of similar triangles have the same ratio as corresponding sides.
Medians of similar triangles are proportional to corresponding sides.
A transformation that enlarges or reduces a figure by a scale factor from a center point.
A combination of rigid motions and a dilation.
The fixed point from which a dilation is performed.
Enlargement — the image is larger than the pre-image.
Reduction — the image is smaller than the pre-image.
A drawing in which all dimensions are proportional to the actual object.
The ratio of a length in the drawing to the actual length (e.g., 1 cm : 10 m).
A three-dimensional representation at a consistent scale.
Architects, engineers, and mapmakers use scale drawings.
Multiply drawing measurement by the scale factor to find actual dimension.
A geometric figure where each part is a reduced-scale copy of the whole.
A property where parts of a figure are similar to the whole.
The process of repeating a set of rules to generate a fractal.
A fractal made by repeatedly removing the center triangle from equilateral triangles.
A fractal made by adding equilateral triangles to each side of a figure, infinitely.
For a and b: GM = √(ab); used in right triangle altitude relationships.
The altitude to the hypotenuse creates two triangles similar to each other and to the original.
Each leg is the geometric mean of the hypotenuse and its adjacent segment.
The altitude is the geometric mean of the two segments of the hypotenuse.
Finding missing lengths in right triangles using proportions.

u8_l8.2.title: The Pythagorean Theorem and Its Converse

flashcards.definition: a² + b² = c²; relates the legs and hypotenuse of a right triangle.
The longest side of a right triangle, opposite the right angle.
Three positive integers a, b, c where a² + b² = c² (e.g., 3-4-5, 5-12-13).
If a² + b² = c², the triangle is right. If a² + b² > c², acute. If <, obtuse.
Derived from the Pythagorean Theorem applied to coordinate geometry.
Legs are congruent; hypotenuse = leg × √2.
Sides in ratio 1 : √3 : 2 (short leg : long leg : hypotenuse).
Opposite the 30° angle; hypotenuse = 2 × short leg.
Opposite the 60° angle; long leg = short leg × √3.
These triangles appear in equilateral triangle splits and square diagonal cuts.
sin θ = opposite / hypotenuse.
cos θ = adjacent / hypotenuse.
tan θ = opposite / adjacent.
Mnemonic: Sine = Opposite/Hypotenuse, Cosine = Adjacent/Hypotenuse, Tangent = Opposite/Adjacent.
sin⁻¹, cos⁻¹, tan⁻¹ find the angle given a ratio.
The angle formed from the horizontal looking upward to an object.
The angle formed from the horizontal looking downward to an object.
Use trig ratios to find heights or distances from angles of elevation/depression.
Angle of elevation from one point equals angle of depression from the other (alternate interior).
The reference line from which elevation and depression are measured.

u8_l8.6.title: The Law of Sines and Cosines

flashcards.definition: a/sin A = b/sin B = c/sin C; used for non-right triangles.
c² = a² + b² − 2ab cos C; generalizes the Pythagorean theorem.
May have 0, 1, or 2 triangle solutions when using Law of Sines.
Use Law of Sines (AAS, ASA) or Law of Cosines (SAS, SSS).
Area = ½ ab sin C; uses two sides and the included angle.
A quantity with both magnitude (size) and direction.
The length of a vector; |v| = √(x² + y²).
The angle a vector makes with the positive x-axis.
A vector written as ⟨x, y⟩.
⟨a, b⟩ + ⟨c, d⟩ = ⟨a+c, b+d⟩; graphically: tip-to-tail method.
(r, θ) where r is distance from origin and θ is angle from positive x-axis.
x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ.
r = √(x² + y²), θ = arctan(y/x).
a + bi; plotted on the complex plane (real axis, imaginary axis).
z = r(cos θ + i sin θ) = r cis θ.
A transformation that flips a figure over a line of reflection.
The 'mirror line'; every point and its image are equidistant from this line.
A transformation that slides every point the same distance and direction.
⟨a, b⟩ describes the horizontal and vertical shift.
A transformation that preserves distance (reflections, translations, rotations).
Pre-image: the original figure. Image: the transformed figure (prime notation: A').
A transformation that turns a figure around a center point.
The fixed point around which the figure rotates.
Performing two or more transformations in sequence.
A translation followed by a reflection along the direction of translation.
Equivalent to a single translation.
Equivalent to a single rotation.
The order of transformations can affect the final image.
A figure has line symmetry if a line can divide it into two mirror-image halves.
A figure maps onto itself after a rotation of less than 360°.
The number of times a figure maps onto itself during a 360° rotation.
Rotational symmetry of order 2 (180° rotation maps figure to itself).
The line of reflection for line symmetry.
A transformation that resizes a figure by a scale factor from a center.
k > 1: enlargement. 0 < k < 1: reduction. k = 1: congruent.
The fixed point from which distances are measured and scaled.
(x, y) → (kx, ky) for dilation centered at the origin.
Dilations produce similar figures (same shape, proportional sides).

u9_l9.7.title: Transformations in the Coordinate Plane ⭐

flashcards.definition: T: (x, y) → (x', y'); describes the rule mapping each point.
All transformations can be described with coordinate rules.
Transformations can be expressed as matrix multiplications.
Translations and rotations preserve orientation; reflections reverse it.
Pairing each point of the pre-image with its corresponding image point.
